      SEC v. Crypto
   =20
 =20

=E2=80=9CA decent rule of thumb,=E2=80=9D   I wrote in March, =E2=80=9Cis t=
hat all cryptocurrency exchanges are doing crimes, and if you=E2=80=99re lu=
cky your exchange is doing only process crimes.=E2=80=9D Like:
=09Is your exchange operating an illegal securities exchange in the US? Yes=
! Yes it is! The view of the US Securities and Exchange Commission, at leas=
t, is that every crypto exchange in the US is illegal. You might disagree =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0plenty of crypto exchange executives disagree, and we will t=
alk more about the arguments below=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0but realistically, i=
f you are trading crypto, you simply cannot be too squeamish about strict a=
dherence to US securities law.=09Is your exchange stealing all of its custo=
mers=E2=80=99 money? It might be! Some are! Others are not!=C2=A0This is th=
e one you should mostly care about, if you are a customer.

These things are not especially correlated because, again,=C2=A0every crypt=
o exchange is violating US securities law. =E2=80=9COooh I shouldn=E2=80=99=
t trust my money to those guys because they are violating US law=E2=80=9D: =
Sure, yes, a reasonable position that would save you from a lot of crypto d=
isasters, but also one that would prevent you from trading crypto entirely.=
 Your choice!
I am overstating all of this but=C2=A0not by much, man, not by much.
Yesterday the SEC=C2=A0sued Binance, the biggest global crypto exchange, an=
d its founder Changpeng Zhao, for operating an illegal securities exchange.=
 Today the SEC  sued Coinbase Inc., the biggest US crypto exchange, for ope=
rating an illegal securities exchange.
There are basically two ways for a crypto exchange to get in trouble with t=
he SEC. The=C2=A0good=C2=A0way is that you get in trouble for running an il=
legal securities exchange. In April, the SEC   sued Bittrex Inc.=C2=A0for a=
llegedly operating an illegal securities exchange; any reasonable reading o=
f the Bittrex case made it clear that similar cases were coming against Coi=
nbase and Binance. Just being a crypto exchange in the US is, in the SEC=E2=
=80=99s eyes, illegal.
The=C2=A0bad=C2=A0way is that you get in trouble for stealing all the money=
. Last December,   the SEC sued FTX Trading Ltd., a big crypto exchange.=C2=
=A0Here is  the SEC=E2=80=99s complaint against FTX. I am=C2=A0absolutely c=
ertain=C2=A0that the SEC thinks that FTX operated an illegal securities exc=
hange in the US. But that does not even=C2=A0come up in the complaint.=C2=
=A0There is too much else going on.=C2=A0FTX allegedly stole all the money!=
 When an exchange steals all the money, the SEC focuses on that. When it do=
esn=E2=80=99t steal all the money, the SEC focuses on the illegal securitie=
s exchange stuff.
And so one question about this week=E2=80=99s cases is: Is the SEC suing Co=
inbase and Binance for being crypto exchanges, or for being bad=C2=A0crypto=
 exchanges? Is the claim here =E2=80=9Cyou let people trade crypto, which w=
e think is illegal,=E2=80=9D or is it =E2=80=9Cyou let people trade crypto =
and steal their money=E2=80=9D?
With Coinbase, I think=C2=A0the answer is obvious. Coinbase is, as crypto e=
xchanges go, quite law-abiding. It is a US public company, incorporated in =
Delaware, listed on the Nasdaq. It went public in a direct listing in 2021,=
 filing extensive disclosures with the SEC. Its financial statements are au=
dited by Deloitte & Touche. Its   business model seems to consist of taking=
 money from customers, using the money to buy crypto, and keeping the crypt=
o somewhere safe with the customers=E2=80=99 names on it. I hesitate to mak=
e any bold claims about any crypto actors, and I have been wrong before, bu=
t it is my impression that Coinbase does not steal the money.
And in fact  the SEC=E2=80=99s complaint against Coinbase is very dry and f=
ocused entirely on the fact that Coinbase did not register as a securities =
exchange. Again, in the SEC=E2=80=99s view, every crypto exchange is violat=
ing US securities law. But Coinbase is doing so politely and, relatively sp=
eaking, harmlessly. Not=C2=A0totally=C2=A0harmlessly =E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=
=9CCoinbase=E2=80=99s failure to register has deprived investors of signifi=
cant protections, including inspection by the SEC, recordkeeping requiremen=
ts, and safeguards against conflicts of interest,=E2=80=9D  says the SEC =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0but relatively.
With Binance, the answer is more interesting. Binance is, as crypto exchang=
es go, sort of an average amount of law-abiding? It is notoriously opaque, =
headquartered nowhere, a web of confusing entities designed to avoid regula=
tion. (The SEC quotes its chief compliance officer saying, in 2018, =E2=80=
=9Cwe do not want [Binance].com to be regulated ever.=E2=80=9D) Like FTX, i=
t has its own token, called BNB; it has its own affiliated trading firms; i=
t has separate platforms for US customers (Binance.us) and for the rest of =
the world (Binance.com). It was   sued by the US Commodity Futures Trading =
Commission in March, mostly for letting big US customers (high-frequency ma=
rket makers like=C2=A0Jane Street and Tower Research) trade on its Binance.=
com exchange through their offshore affiliates, though there are not=C2=A0z=
ero=C2=A0mentions of terrorist financing in the CFTC complaint.=C2=A0
And similarly  the SEC=E2=80=99s complaint against Binance includes=C2=A0so=
me=C2=A0claims that Binance was doing bad stuff. Binance has some affiliate=
d market makers =E2=80=94=C2=A0firms including Sigma Chain AG and Merit Pea=
k Ltd. that are allegedly controlled by Zhao and that traded on Binance.com=
 and Binance.us =E2=80=94 and the SEC hints that they got up to shady stuff=
:
For example, by 2021, at least $145 million was transferred from BAM Tradin=
g [i.e. Binance.us]=C2=A0to a Sigma Chain account, and another $45 million =
of funds were transferred from BAM Trading=E2=80=99s Trust Company B accoun=
t to the Sigma Chain account. From this account, Sigma Chain spent $11 mill=
ion to purchase a yacht.
And:
From at least September 2019 until June 2022, Sigma Chain AG (=E2=80=9CSigm=
a Chain=E2=80=9D), a trading firm owned and controlled by Zhao, engaged in =
wash trading that artificially inflated the trading volume of crypto asset =
securities on the Binance.US Platform.=C2=A0
And more generally:
The SEC also alleges that Zhao and Binance exercise control of the platform=
s=E2=80=99 customers=E2=80=99 assets, permitting them to commingle customer=

But the SEC does not dwell much on these claims,

 and for the most part the Binance complaint is the same as the Coinbase co=
mplaint: Binance is accused of operating a crypto exchange that was open to=
 US customers and that listed crypto tokens that are securities, without re=
gistering as a US securities exchange.=C2=A0I am tempted to read yesterday=
=E2=80=99s lawsuit as kind of an endorsement=C2=A0of Binance by the SEC. Th=
e SEC, and before it the CFTC, investigated Binance carefully and wrote a 1=
36-page complaint about every bad thing it could find, and all it could fin=

Still, while the arguments in the two complaints are mostly the same, Coinb=

Recognizing that at least certain crypto assets were being offered, sold, a=

Very responsible, though the SEC disagrees with Coinbase=E2=80=99s conclusi=

Between late 2019 and the end of 2020, Coinbase more than doubled the numbe=

      SEC v. Crypto: What is a security?
   =20
 =20

Okay let=E2=80=99s discuss the basic theory of the SEC=E2=80=99s case here,=
 which we previously   discussed when the SEC sued Bittrex:
=09If you run an exchange that offers securities trading in the US, you nee=
d to register it as a securities exchange with the SEC.=09Binance and Coinb=
ase offer trading of crypto tokens that are securities.=09They did not regi=
ster their US exchanges as securities exchanges.
  [2]

=09Bad!

Point 1 is a bit more nuanced than that; there are, for instance, stock tra=
ding venues that are not registered as securities exchanges, though they ar=
e registered with the SEC under other rules. But from the SEC=E2=80=99s per=
spective the important thing here is that the rules for securities exchange=
s are designed to protect investors. In particular, they tend to require a =
separation of three key functions =E2=80=94=C2=A0the=C2=A0exchange=C2=A0tha=
t matches buyers and sellers, the=C2=A0broker-dealer=C2=A0that trades on th=
e exchange on behalf of customers, and the clearinghouse that actually move=
s the money and securities =E2=80=94=C2=A0that are usually combined in cryp=
to. In the stock market, you go to Robinhood=E2=80=99s website to put in an=
 order to buy stock on the New York Stock Exchange,
  [3]
 and the Depository Trust Co. keeps custody of the stock and settles the tr=
ade. In the crypto market, you go to Coinbase=E2=80=99s website to put in a=
n order to buy crypto on Coinbase, and Coinbase keeps custody of the crypto=
 and settles the trade.=C2=A0
But the main point here is Point 3: Are crypto tokens securities?
  [4]
=C2=A0The SEC=E2=80=99s basic view is that most crypto tokens =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0not all of them, not Bitcoin, but most of them =E2=80=94=C2=A0are securi=
ties under US law. Coinbase=E2=80=99s view, certainly, is that lots of them=
 aren=E2=80=99t. The particular question at issue here is whether a list of=
 popular crypto tokens =E2=80=94=C2=A0the SEC cites a bunch including Solan=
a=E2=80=99s SOL token, Cardano=E2=80=99s ADA, Polygon=E2=80=99s MATIC, File=
coin=E2=80=99s FIL, Decentraland=E2=80=99s MANA, Algorand=E2=80=99s ALGO, A=
xie Infinity=E2=80=99s AXS and Voyager Digital=E2=80=99s VGX =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0that were listed on Binance and/or Coinbase are securities.
  [5]

The  US securities laws define a =E2=80=9Csecurity=E2=80=9D to include, amo=
ng other things,=C2=A0any =E2=80=9Cstock,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9Ccertificate of =
interest or participation in any profit-sharing agreement,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=
=9Cpreorganization certificate or subscription,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9Ctransfera=
ble share, investment contract, [or] voting-trust=C2=A0certificate.=E2=80=
=9D The most general term there is =E2=80=9Cinvestment contract,=E2=80=9D a=
nd the US Supreme Court explained it in a famous 1946 case called  SEC v. W=
.J. Howey Co.:

An investment contract, for purposes of the Securities Act, means a contrac=
t, transaction=C2=A0or scheme whereby a person invests his money in a commo=
n enterprise and is led to expect profits solely from the efforts of the pr=
omoter or a third party, it being immaterial whether the shares in the ente=
rprise are evidenced by formal certificates or by nominal interests in the =
physical assets employed in the enterprise. Such a definition =E2=80=A6 per=
mits the fulfillment of the statutory purpose of compelling full and fair d=
isclosure relative to the issuance of =E2=80=9Cthe many types of instrument=
s that, in our commercial world, fall within the ordinary concept of a secu=
rity.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6 It embodies a flexible, rather than a static, prin=
ciple, one that is capable of adaptation to meet the countless and variable=
 schemes devised by those who seek the use of the money of others on the pr=
omise of profits. =E2=80=A6
The investors provide the capital and share in the earnings and profits; th=
e promoters manage, control, and operate the enterprise. It follows that th=
e arrangements whereby the investors' interests are made manifest involve i=
nvestment contracts, regardless of the legal terminology in which such cont=
racts are clothed. =E2=80=A6
The test is whether the scheme involves an investment of money in a common =
enterprise with profits to come solely from the efforts of others. If that =
test be satisfied, it is immaterial whether the enterprise is speculative o=
r nonspeculative, or whether there is a sale of property with or without in=
trinsic value.

This has become the =E2=80=9CHowey test,=E2=80=9D and courts ask whether th=
ere is (1) an investment of money (2) in a common enterprise (3) with an ex=
pectation of profits (4) to come solely from the efforts of others. The SEC=
  has argued, at least  since 2017, that most crypto enterprises fit this d=
escription.
For instance, consider Solana. Solana is a blockchain that runs crypto appl=
ications; its native token is called SOL.=C2=A0Here=E2=80=99s the SEC=E2=80=
=99s explanation of Solana
  [6]
:

=E2=80=9CSOL=E2=80=9D is the native token of the Solana blockchain. The Sol=
ana blockchain was created by Solana Labs, Inc. (=E2=80=9CSolana Labs=E2=80=
=9D), a Delaware corporation headquartered in San Francisco that was founde=
d in 2018 by Anatoly Yakovenko (=E2=80=9CYakovenko=E2=80=9D) and Raj Gokal =
(Solana Labs=E2=80=99 current CEO and COO, respectively). According to Sola=
na=E2=80=99s website, www.solana.com, the Solana blockchain is a network up=
on which decentralized apps (=E2=80=9CdApps=E2=80=9D) can be built, and is =
comprised of a platform that aims to improve blockchain scalability and ach=
ieve high transaction speeds by using a combination of consensus mechanisms=
.
According to Solana=E2=80=99s website, SOL may be =E2=80=9Cstaked=E2=80=9D =
on the Solana blockchain to earn rewards, and a certain infinitesimal amoun=
t of SOL must be =E2=80=9Cburned=E2=80=9D to propose a transaction on the S=
olana blockchain, a common function for native tokens on blockchains that c=
onstitutes a method for cryptographically distributed ledgers to avoid a po=
tential bad actor from =E2=80=9Cspamming=E2=80=9D a blockchain by overwhelm=
ing it with an infinite number of proposed transactions.

Solana Labs sold SOL tokens to raise money to build the Solana ecosystem:
Solana Labs stated publicly that it would pool the proceeds from its privat=
e and public SOL sales in omnibus crypto asset wallets that it controlled, =
and that it would use those proceeds to fund the development, operations, a=
nd marketing efforts with respect to the Solana blockchain in order to attr=
act more users to that blockchain (potentially increasing the demand for,=
=C2=A0and therefore the value of, SOL itself, given the need for those who =
wish to interact with the Solana blockchain to tender SOL). For example, in=
 connection with the 2021 private sale of SOL, Solana Labs stated publicly =
that it would use investor funds to: (i) hire engineers and support staff t=
o help grow Solana=E2=80=99s developer ecosystem; (ii) =E2=80=9Caccelerate =
the deployment of market-ready applications focused on onboarding the next =
billion users into crypto=E2=80=9D; (iii) =E2=80=9Claunch an incubation stu=
dio to accelerate the development of decentralized applications and Platfor=
ms building on Solana=E2=80=9D; and (iv) develop a =E2=80=9Cventure investi=
ng arm=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Ctrading desk dedicated to the Solana ecosystem=
.=E2=80=9D
The Howey test:
=09Did investors invest money? Yes, SOL was sold for money, to raise fundin=
g to build Solana.
  [7]

=09Was there a common enterprise? Yes, Solana is an=C2=A0enterprise; it is =
a blockchain ecosystem that competes with Ethereum and Cardano and so forth=
, that tries to attract users.=09Is there an expectation of profits? Yes, p=
eople bought SOL hoping that it would go up, and it did.=C2=A0=09Do the pro=
fits come from the efforts of others? Yes, SOL went up because its promoter=
s and developers built Solana into a popular blockchain, as they said they =
would, increasing the demand for SOL.

These are often close questions. Most big crypto blockchains are to some ex=
tent decentralized; Solana=E2=80=99s growth depends not only on the efforts=
 of Solana Labs but also on the efforts of third-party users and developers=
 who like using it.
  [8]
 With some crypto tokens, it is plausible to argue that people buy the toke=
ns=C2=A0not=C2=A0as an investment with an expectation of profits, but rathe=
r to pay for transactions on the blockchain; the SOL token is the =E2=80=9C=
gas=E2=80=9D that people use to run programs and transactions on the Solana=
 blockchain, and if you buy SOL as a pure =E2=80=9Cutility token=E2=80=9D t=
hen it is arguably not a security. Most crypto tokens   have=C2=A0both=C2=
=A0utility=C2=A0and=C2=A0speculative-investment=C2=A0aspects, muddying the =
analysis a bit.
Also, even if you buy SOL as a speculative investment, it is not clear that=
 you are buying it to=C2=A0share in the profits of the underlying enterpris=
e.=C2=A0Your thought process might be =E2=80=9Cif I buy SOL, a lot of other=
 people might buy SOL, and its price will go up and I will make money.=E2=
=80=9D That is not really an expectation of profits derived from the =E2=80=
=9Cefforts of others=E2=80=9D; that=E2=80=99s an expectation of profits der=
ived from speculative mania and online memes. We   talked yesterday about D=
ogecoin, a joke crypto token where everyone pretty ostentatiously promised =
to do nothing at all to build an ecosystem; people buy Dogecoin because the=
y think other people will buy Dogecoin. I=C2=A0think=C2=A0that that means t=
hat Dogecoin is not a security; a purely meaningless token doesn=E2=80=99t =
involve enough efforts-of-others to qualify.
  [9]
=C2=A0(Surely, e.g., Beanie Babies are not securities.) And surely even whe=
n a crypto project=C2=A0does=C2=A0promise efforts and ecosystems and hard-w=
orking brilliant developers, a lot of people probably buy the tokens for th=
e same pure number-go-up reasons that they bought Dogecoin.=C2=A0
But the way that I like to think of crypto and securities is that most cryp=
to tokens are pretty clearly analogues of=C2=A0stock=C2=A0in some underlyin=
g=C2=A0tech business. In Solana (and Cardano, Polygon, etc.), the underlyin=
g business is a platform business, a blockchain ecosystem for building apps=
, largely though not exclusively financial-services apps for trading=C2=A0c=
rypto. And everyone who buys the tokens is in some loose sense a quasi-shar=
eholder of the business. And they are rewarded in the same way that traditi=
onal shareholders are: with stock buybacks.
  [10]
=C2=A0The crypto term for stock buybacks is =E2=80=9Cburning=E2=80=9D:
Further, Solana Labs markets that it =E2=80=9Cburns=E2=80=9D (or destroys) =
SOL tokens as part of a =E2=80=9Cdeflationary model.=E2=80=9D As Yakovenko =
explained in an April 14, 2021 article entitled =E2=80=9CSolana (SOL): Scal=
ing Crypto to the Masses=E2=80=9D posted on gemini.com, =E2=80=9CSolana tra=
nsaction fees are paid in SOL and=C2=A0burnt (or permanently destroyed) as =
a deflationary mechanism to reduce the total supply and thereby maintain a =
healthy SOL price.=E2=80=9D As explained on the Solana website, since the S=
olana network was launched, the =E2=80=9CTotal Current Supply=E2=80=9D of S=
OL =E2=80=9Chas been reduced by the burning of transaction fees and a plann=
ed token reduction event.=E2=80=9D This marketed burning of SOL as part of =
the Solana network=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cdeflationary mechanism=E2=80=9D has l=
ed investors reasonably to view their purchase of SOL as having the potenti=
al for profit to the extent there is a built-in mechanism to decrease the s=
upply and therefore increase the price of SOL.
In the  Binance complaint, the SEC quotes Changpeng Zhao describing the bur=
ning mechanism for Binance=E2=80=99s own BNB token:=C2=A0
Indeed, on July 9, 2019, Zhao described Binance=E2=80=99s planned BNB burn =
in an interview posted on YouTube, stating that a =E2=80=9Cbenefit we promi=
sed in the Whitepaper is every quarter we will use 20 percent of our profit=
s to buy back [BNB] at the market value =E2=80=A6 we will buy back and dest=
roy those. We will destroy up to 100 million Binance coins. Basically half =
of all available coins =E2=80=A6 Financially that works the same way as a d=
ividend economically.=E2=80=9D
I mean I would say it works the same as a stock buyback, but sure, dividend=
s and stock buybacks are essentially equivalent. The point is that sharehol=
ders =E2=80=94=C2=A0sorry, tokenholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0in a crypto project =
share in the profits of that project when it uses some of its revenue to bu=
y back and burn tokens, increasing the value of the remaining tokens, exact=
ly the way a share buyback works.
One way to understand crypto economics is that crypto built a new way to se=
ll stock=C2=A0in promising technology and financial businesses=C2=A0without=
 calling it stock. If you are starting a crypto exchange, for instance, and=
 you want to raise money, you could go to investors and offer them stock in=
 your business. If the business does well, there will be lots of profits (f=
rom charging customers trading fees), and you will share them with your inv=
estors. But this has problems:
=09If you sell stock to the public, you will need to register with the SEC.=
=09If you sell stock to big venture capitalists,=C2=A0they will need to reg=
ister their resales with the SEC, or otherwise find an exemption from SEC r=
egistration.=09Either way you will probably have to give your investors=C2=
=A0some=C2=A0sort of financial information about the business to get their =
money.=09Your shareholders might want voting rights, ongoing financial disc=
losures, etc., which are customary and often mandated by law.

Or, if you are starting a crypto exchange, you can go to investors and offe=
r them=C2=A0tokens=C2=A0in your business. If the business does well, there =
will be lots of profits (from charging customers trading fees), and you wil=
l share them with your investors (by buying and burning tokens). This is ve=
ry nice:
=09If you sell tokens to the public, you can say that they are not securiti=
es, and not register with the SEC.=09If you sell the tokens to big VCS,=C2=
=A0they=C2=A0can say that they are not securities, and resell them freely.=
=09You will write a white paper to sell the tokens that does not necessaril=
y get into a ton of financial or operational detail.=09You can give the tok=
ens whatever rights you want.

I am being a little unfair. I am describing a pure regulatory arbitrage; Bi=
nance=E2=80=99s BNB token =E2=80=94=C2=A0or   FTX=E2=80=99s FTT token =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0is a pure substitute for stock, issued by a company to raise mo=
ney to run its centralized business. Lots of crypto is not quite like this;=
 in some projects   there is a real idealism about building decentralized e=
cosystems that will not be owned by any one company, and selling tokens can=
 be a way to create and fund projects without owners, an economic model tha=
t really is very different from a shareholder-owned corporation. But much o=
f the time, the crypto ecosystems seem to be built by pretty centralized te=
ams, and the tokens are sold as more or less stock in a new tech company wi=
th a promising idea started by a promising team.
You can see why people in crypto like this! It combines regulatory arbitrag=
e with a certain exciting philosophical newfangledness. You can see why the=
 SEC does not like it! The SEC knows all about =E2=80=9C=C2=A0the countless=
 and variable schemes devised by those who seek the use of the money of oth=
ers on the promise of profits.=E2=80=9D The SEC is the regulator getting ar=
bitraged. And it is not happy about it.

      SEC v. Crypto: What now?
   =20
 =20

Oh I don=E2=80=99t know. Conceptually, there are several possible outcomes =
here:
=09The SEC wins and crypto is more or less banned in the US. You can still =
buy Bitcoin and probably Ethereum
  [11]
=C2=A0and maybe Dogecoin in the US, because those are not securities, but a=
ny other crypto project is probably a security and not going to be offered =
for trading in the US. Crypto withers and dies and everyone moves on to art=
ificial intelligence. The SEC kills crypto, as a sort of slow-burn revenge =
for crypto=E2=80=99s attempt to arbitrage around the SEC.=09Same, except th=
at crypto develops and flourishes elsewhere, and the US simply misses it. C=
rypto turns out to be extremely world-changing and valuable, and the US is =
left behind. Or it turns out to be a weird niche financial product you can =
trade in Europe but not in the US, like  binary options or contracts for di=
fferences. Either way it lives on abroad, but not in the US.=09The SEC wins=
, and then somebody =E2=80=94=C2=A0some combination of existing crypto firm=
s, new crypto entrants and=C2=A0legacy financial services firms =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0finds a path forward for crypto to be traded in the US in compliance =
with US securities laws. Everyone buckles down and says =E2=80=9Cokay, Sola=
na is going to start filing annual reports with audited financial statement=
s,=E2=80=9D and people will start crypto exchanges that register with the S=
EC and that are separate from the clearinghouses and brokerages, etc. This =
seems=C2=A0very=C2=A0hard, because the SEC is quite   clearly not intereste=
d in   accommodating any crypto projects. I am not going to sit here and te=
ll you =E2=80=9Chere=E2=80=99s how crypto firms can register their tokens a=
s securities.=E2=80=9D Certainly Coinbase has been trying forever to figure=
 out how to do it =E2=80=94=C2=A0they have  pestered the SEC for rules allo=
wing it, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0with, so far, not much luck. But I suppose it=
=E2=80=99s always possible.
  [12]

=09The SEC loses, the courts say =E2=80=9Cwhat, no, none of this stuff is a=
 security,=E2=80=9D and crypto continues to trade in the US without much se=
curities regulation.=09Congress (or a future SEC) steps in to change the ru=
les, saying =E2=80=9Cwell sure all this stuff is technically illegal under =
existing law, but it is crazy to stifle innovation like this, so we will ma=
ke new rules to allow for regulated trading=C2=A0of crypto in the US.=E2=80=
=9D=C2=A0

I don=E2=80=99t know which outcome I would bet on. The last outcome is the =
one that the crypto industry wants, and there does seem to be some=C2=A0app=
etite in Congress to write crypto rules.
But I do want to say that the SEC is clearly betting on the first outcome. =
That is why it is bringing these cases=C2=A0now, after the collapse of FTX =
and so many other big crypto firms, after crypto prices have fallen, after =
the venture capitalists have all moved on to AI. These cases, against Binan=
ce and Coinbase, are high-risk cases for the SEC: Coinbase and Bitcoin are =
big well-funded companies=C2=A0with good lawyers and lobbyists,=C2=A0they h=
ave the resources and motivation to fight these cases to the end, and they =
do have decent legal arguments. The SEC might lose! But it is being strateg=
ic about maximizing its chances. I   wrote in February:

When crypto is popular and exciting and going up, if you are a regulator wh=
o says =E2=80=9Cno, we must stop this,=E2=80=9D you look like a killjoy. In=
vestors want to put their money into stuff that is going up, and they are m=
ad at you for stopping them. Politicians like the stuff that is going up, a=
nd hold hearings about how you=E2=80=99re stifling innovation. Crypto found=
ers are rich and popular and criticize you on Twitter and get a lot of like=
s and retweets. Your own regulatory employees, who have an eye on their nex=
t private-sector jobs, want to be leaders in crypto innovation rather than =
just banning everything.
When crypto is going down and so many projects are evaporating in fraud and=
 bankruptcy, you can kind of say =E2=80=9CI told you so.=E2=80=9D There is =
just a lot more appetite to regulate, or I guess just to shut everything do=
wn. =E2=80=9CYou are stifling innovation,=E2=80=9D the indicted founder of =
a bankrupt crypto firm can say, but nobody cares.=C2=A0

That is the bet that the SEC is making. Now we=E2=80=99ll see if it=E2=80=
=99s right.

      Private gigs
   =20
 =20

If you work at a private equity firm and it has reached its 25th anniversar=
y, it will probably have a party to celebrate. If it has a party, it will p=
robably book a musical guest. If it books a musical guest and David Solomon=
 is unavailable, it will probably book Flo Rida. You will go to your privat=
e equity firm=E2=80=99s anniversary party and your boss will give a speech =
about the value you provide to limited partners, and then she will say =E2=
=80=9Cand now, Flo Rida.=E2=80=9D And then Flo Rida will come out on stage,=
 and you will perhaps, for just a second, think, =E2=80=9Cwell, why Flo Rid=
a?=E2=80=9D And he will probably be thinking =E2=80=9Cwell, why this privat=
e equity firm?=E2=80=9D But that is just life in modern capitalism, sometim=
es private equity firms celebrate significant milestones and Flo Rida is th=
ere. It doesn=E2=80=99t have to make sense; it just is.
Evan Osnos has=C2=A0an extremely delightful New Yorker article about the pr=
ivate gig industry, in which star and former-star and near-star musicians c=
ollect large checks for playing short sets for the bar mitzvahs and corpora=
te parties of the wealthy. =E2=80=9CIf you can say, =E2=80=98Hey, I=E2=80=
=99m going to go have a bad time for an afternoon, but it=E2=80=99s going t=
o pay for my kid=E2=80=99s entire college education,=E2=80=99 then that=E2=
=80=99s a trade-off I think most responsible adults will make,=E2=80=9D say=
s one booking agent, correctly. Osnos follows Flo Rida, who =E2=80=9Cattain=
ed celebrity in 2008 with his song =E2=80=98Low,=E2=80=99 an admiring ode t=
o a Rubenesque beauty on the dance floor,=E2=80=9D to both a bar mitzvah an=
d a private equity gig:

On a crisp spring night on the southern tip of Manhattan, Flo Rida was back=
stage once more. This time, he huddled in a darkened hallway with his dance=
rs and the d.j. and Int=E2=80=99l Nephew, their arms laced around one anoth=
er, as they murmured a prayer. Then they lined up in a loose stack behind t=
he door that separated them from the stage.=C2=A0
On the other side, in a cavernous ballroom bathed in purple light, about fo=
ur hundred people with matching lanyards were celebrating the twenty-fifth =
anniversary of a private-equity firm. It was business casual, with the occa=
sional bow tie and ball gown. A sushi buffet ran the length of one wall. Th=
e venue, Casa Cipriani, occupies a restored Beaux-Arts ferry terminal that =
also includes a hotel and private club, which offers suites with cashmere-l=
ined walls, easy access to a helicopter pad, and fastidious attention to se=
rvice. Guests are asked to choose which type of Italian bedding they prefer=
, cotton or linen.
At the lectern, two of the firm=E2=80=99s leaders were holding forth. A gra=
y-haired man in a gray plaid blazer praised his colleagues=E2=80=99 deliver=
y of =E2=80=9Cindustry-leading returns for our limited partners,=E2=80=9D a=
nd thanked attendees for various feats, including =E2=80=9Cfive hundred mil=
lion in proceeds at a 5.5x multiple on investment capital=E2=80=9D and endu=
ring =E2=80=9Cawful six-hour golf rounds with me.=E2=80=9D As showtime appr=
oached, a partner in an elegant black-and-white gown told the crowd, =E2=80=
=9CWith pride in our hearts and sushi in our mouths, let=E2=80=99s celebrat=
e.=E2=80=9D
The partygoers had not been told who the headline entertainer would be, and=
 some were quietly hoping for Springsteen. When Flo charged out from backst=
age, there was a brief vacuum of silence. But he plunged ahead into =E2=80=
=9CGood Feeling,=E2=80=9D a thumping ode to success, with references to a G=
ulfstream, a Bugatti, and a Maybach. (=E2=80=9CTalk like a winner, my chest=
 to that sun / G5 dealer, U.S. to Taiwan.=E2=80=9D) The crowd began to fall=
 in, and Flo showed a rowdier mode than he had at the bar mitzvah. =E2=80=
=9CWho wants some shots?=E2=80=9D he shouted, holding up bottles of vodka a=
nd tequila. There was a nervous titter; a gentleman in a bow tie craned his=
 neck for a look, and then some of the younger guests stepped forward to dr=
ink from bottles held in Flo=E2=80=99s outstretched hands. The mood in the =
room started to evolve fast.

Springsteen! The main thing that I took away from this is that Flo Rida=E2=
=80=99s band includes =E2=80=9Ca younger rapper known as Int=E2=80=99l Neph=
ew,=E2=80=9D the greatest stage name I have ever seen, but I will probably =
also use the =E2=80=9Cwith pride in our hearts and sushi in our mouths=E2=
=80=9D line in the future.

  [1] In particular the yacht is just mentioned and dropped. There are weir=
d claims that *could* be read as ominous: =E2=80=9CSince the launch of the =
Binance.US Platform, Merit Peak=E2=80=99s U.S. bank account received, as a =
=E2=80=98pass through=E2=80=99 account, over $20 billion that included cust=
omer funds from both Binance Platforms. Merit Peak then transferred the vas=
t majority of those funds to Trust Company A, in transfers that appear to r=
elate to the purchase of BUSD. As Merit Peak was a purportedly independent =
entity, sending Binance customer funds to Merit Peak placed those funds at =
risk, including of loss or theft, and was done without notice to customers.=
=E2=80=9D I think I read that to mean =E2=80=9CMerit Peak operated as a mar=
ket maker selling Binance customers BUSD stablecoins,=E2=80=9D rather than =
=E2=80=9CBinance siphoned $20 billion of customer money to its affiliated t=
rading firm,=E2=80=9D but the SEC just mentions this and moves on.


  [2] Coinbase is a US exchange so the analysis is pretty simple. Binance h=
as Binance.us, which is open to US customers, and Binance.com, which is not=
. The SEC=E2=80=99s complaint against Binance has two components: One is th=
at Binance.us is an illegal securities exchange, because it offers spot tra=
ding in crypto assets that the SEC claims are securities (same as Coinbase)=
; the other is that Binance.com actually did let big US traders use its pla=
tform (same as the CFTC case against Binance).=20


  [3] Actually Robinhood almost certainly=20
routes your order to a wholesaler to execute, rather than sending it to the=
 exchange; to some extent Robinhood, or the wholesaler, runs a venue for ma=
tching buy and sell orders, with Robinhood=E2=80=99s retail customers on on=
e side and the wholesaler on the other. This is part of what I mean about s=
tock trading venues not all being registered as exchanges; broker-dealers r=
un various sorts of trading venues (internalizing like this, dark pools, et=
c.) that are somewhat exchange-like but not exchanges. Still they are subje=
ct to more SEC registration than crypto exchanges are.=20


  [4] Another question raised in these cases is: Are crypto *lending* progr=
ams (where a platform like Binance or Coinbase takes its customers=E2=80=99=
 crypto, lends it to crypto hedge funds, charges interest and passes on a p=
ortion of the interest to the customers) and *staking* programs (where the =
platform takes its customers=E2=80=99 crypto, locks it up in blockchain pro=
of-of-stake mechanisms that validate transactions and earn staking rewards,=
 and passes on a portion of the rewards to the customers) securities, even =
if the underlying crypto is not a security? We have talked about this quest=
ion when=20
the SEC sued Kraken for running a staking program, and when it=20
sued Gemini and=20
threatened Coinbase over lending programs. The SEC makes similar claims aga=
inst Coinbase and Binance this week over lending and staking programs.


  [5] The elephant in the room is Ethereum, the second-biggest crypto block=
chain behind Bitcoin, which entirely fits the SEC=E2=80=99s description of =
Solana, Cardano, etc. Is ETH (the Ethereum token) a security?=20
My view is that (1) the SEC *clearly* thinks ETH is a security, (2) for his=
torical reasons the SEC missed that boat and now US law treats ETH as a non=
-security commodity, (3) the SEC doesn=E2=80=99t like it but (4) they=E2=80=
=99re picking their battles and are not going to come out and argue that ET=
H is a security.


  [6] I am quoting the Coinbase complaint, though there is similar text in =
the Binance complaint.


  [7] A side note here. Solana did not, in its early fundraising, sell SOL =
tokens. Instead it sold =E2=80=9CSimple Agreements for Future Tokens,=E2=80=
=9D or SAFTs, that is, a sort of futures-contract wrapper for the SOL token=
s. The thinking here was that the SAFTs were securities, because they were =
used to raise money to build a blockchain ecosystem, but that once that eco=
system was running the tokens would be pure =E2=80=9Cutility tokens,=E2=80=
=9D that is, gas for running the Solana network rather than an investment s=
ecurity. This SAFT idea is sort of best practice in crypto, but the SEC doe=
sn=E2=80=99t really believe in it; their view seems to be once a security, =
always a security. (We talked about this view=20
as far back as 2019.) And so in this week=E2=80=99s complaints, the SEC cit=
es the fact that Solana actually admitted that its (SAFT) fundraising was a=
 securities sale: =E2=80=9CBetween May 2018 and early March 2020, Solana La=
bs filed with the SEC multiple forms claiming that its offers and sales of =
securities=E2=80=94what Solana described in those forms as the =E2=80=98sal=
e and issuance of rights to receive Solana Labs, Inc. tokens in the future =
via a Simple Agreement for Future Tokens (SAFTs)=E2=80=99=E2=80=94were exem=
pt from registration under Rule 506(c) of Regulation D under the Securities=
 Act. Through these offers and sales of securities, Solana sold approximate=
ly 177 million SOL, raising over $23 million.=E2=80=9D You can follow indus=
try best practices in crypto securities-law compliance, but they are still =
not good enough for the SEC.


  [8] Charmingly, a big player in the Solana ecosystem was Sam Bankman-Frie=
d, whoops.


  [9] I am sure some Dogecoin enthusiasts will email me to explain its incr=
edibly powerful ecosystem and road map to global domination.


  [10] Also with staking rewards, which are analogous to dividends. From th=
e SEC=E2=80=99s analysis of Polygon=E2=80=99s MATIC token: =E2=80=9CMATIC h=
olders can earn additional MATIC for staking their MATIC on the Polygon pla=
tform and becoming a validator, from delegating their MATIC to other valida=
tors in return for a portion of the fees collected from validating transact=
ions, or from staking their MATIC with other third parties, such as crypto =
asset platforms that offer staking services.=E2=80=9D


  [11] See footnote 5.


  [12] Last month=20
Prometheum Ember Capital =E2=80=9Csaid it has received the first special pu=
rpose broker-dealer license under US Securities and Exchange Commission gui=
delines that would allow it to custody digital assets securities,=E2=80=9D =
which I guess is a start.

coration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #=
000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  I wrote in M=
arch</a>, =E2=80=9Cis that all cryptocurrency exchanges are doing crimes, a=
nd if you=E2=80=99re lucky your exchange is doing only process crimes.=E2=
=80=9D Like:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Is your exchange operating an illegal=
 securities exchange in the US? Yes! Yes it is! The view of the US Securiti=
es and Exchange Commission, at least, is that every crypto exchange in the =
US is illegal. You might disagree =E2=80=94=C2=A0plenty of crypto exchange =
executives disagree, and we will talk more about the arguments below=C2=A0=
=E2=80=94=C2=A0but realistically, if you are trading crypto, you simply can=
not be too squeamish about strict adherence to US securities law.</li>=09<l=
i style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Is your exchange stealing all of its custom=
ers=E2=80=99 money? It might be! Some are! Others are not!=C2=A0This is the=
 one you should mostly care about, if you are a customer.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">These things are not especially correlated bec=
ause, again,=C2=A0<em>every crypto exchange is violating US securities law<=
/em>. =E2=80=9COooh I shouldn=E2=80=99t trust my money to those guys becaus=
e they are violating US law=E2=80=9D: Sure, yes, a reasonable position that=
 would save you from a lot of crypto disasters, but also one that would pre=
vent you from trading crypto entirely. Your choice!</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I am overstating all of this but=C2=A0<em>not =
by much</em>, man, not by much.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yesterday the SEC=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">With Binance, the answer is more interesting. =

icer saying, in 2018, =E2=80=9Cwe do not want [Binance].com to be regulated=

nce.us) and for the rest of the world (Binance.com). It was <a href=3D"http=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And similarly <a href=3D"https://link.mail.blo=

</a> includes=C2=A0<em>some=C2=A0</em>claims that Binance was doing bad stu=
ff. Binance has some affiliated market makers =E2=80=94=C2=A0firms includin=
g Sigma Chain AG and Merit Peak Ltd. that are allegedly controlled by Zhao =
and that traded on Binance.com and Binance.us =E2=80=94 and the SEC hints t=

<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">For example, by 2021, at least $14=

to a Sigma Chain account, and another $45 million of funds were transferred=
 from BAM Trading=E2=80=99s Trust Company B account to the Sigma Chain acco=

/p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">From at least September 2019 until=
 June 2022, Sigma Chain AG (=E2=80=9CSigma Chain=E2=80=9D), a trading firm =
owned and controlled by Zhao, engaged in wash trading that artificially inf=
lated the trading volume of crypto asset securities on the Binance.US Platf=
orm.=C2=A0</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And more generally:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The SEC also alleges that Zhao and=
 Binance exercise control of the platforms=E2=80=99 customers=E2=80=99 asse=
ts, permitting them to commingle customer assets or divert customer assets =
as they please, including to an entity Zhao owned and controlled called Sig=
ma Chain.=C2=A0</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But the SEC does not dwell much on these claim=
s,<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #000000;=

color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: tra=
nsparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a> and for the most part the Binance complaint is the same as the Coinbas=
e complaint: Binance is accused of operating a crypto exchange that was ope=
n to US customers and that listed crypto tokens that are securities, withou=
t registering as a US securities exchange.=C2=A0I am tempted to read yester=
day=E2=80=99s lawsuit as kind of an <em>e</em><em>ndorsement=C2=A0</em>of B=
inance by the SEC. The SEC, and before it the CFTC, investigated Binance ca=
refully and wrote a 136-page complaint about every bad thing it could find,=
 and all it could find is that Binance is running a crypto exchange.=C2=A0<=
/p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still, while the arguments in the two complain=
ts are mostly the same, Coinbase=E2=80=99s and Binance=E2=80=99s=C2=A0<em>a=
ttitudes </em>are very different. The key legal question, which we=E2=80=99=
ll discuss below, is whether the crypto tokens listed on Binance and Coinba=
se are securities. If they are securities, then probably Coinbase and Binan=
ce (and Bittrex and everyone else) are operating illegal securities exchang=
es; if they are not securities then everything=E2=80=99s fine.=C2=A0Coinbas=
e recognized that this was a potential risk, and set up committees and proc=
edures to ponder and mitigate it. From <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombe=
rgbusiness.com/click/31711038.259416/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9saXRpZ2F0aW=
9uL2NvbXBsYWludHMvMjAyMy9jb21wLXByMjAyMy0xMDIucGRm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23c=
Cfe6d08ee" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank"=
 style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-w=
idth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgr=
ound-color: #ccc;">the SEC=E2=80=99s Coinbase complaint</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Recognizing that at least certain crypto asset=
s were being offered, sold, and otherwise distributed by an identifiable gr=
oup of persons or promoters, in or around September 2018, Coinbase publicly=
 released the =E2=80=9CCoinbase Crypto Asset Framework,=E2=80=9D which incl=
uded a listing application form for crypto asset issuers and promoters seek=
ing to make their crypto assets available on the Coinbase Platform.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Coinbase=E2=80=99s listing application require=
d issuers and promoters to provide information about their crypto assets an=
d blockchain projects. It specifically included requests for information re=
levant to a <em>Howey </em>analysis of the crypto asset. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Additionally, in or around September 2019, Coi=
nbase and other crypto asset businesses founded the Crypto Rating Council (=
=E2=80=9CCRC=E2=80=9D). The CRC subsequently released a framework for analy=
zing crypto assets that =E2=80=9Cdistilled a set of yes or no questions whi=
ch are designed to plainly address each of the four Howey test factors=E2=
=80=9D and assigned to the crypto asset a score ranging from 1 to 5, with a=
 score of 1 indicating that an =E2=80=9Casset has few or no characteristics=
 consistent with treatment as an investment contract,=E2=80=9D and a score =
of 5 meaning that an =E2=80=9Casset has many characteristics strongly consi=
stent with treatment as a security.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In announcing the CRC=E2=80=99s creation, Coin=
base stated, =E2=80=9C[a]lthough the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commissio=
n has issued helpful guidance, whether any given crypto asset is a security=
 ultimately requires a fact-intensive analysis.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Very responsible, though the SEC disagrees wit=
h Coinbase=E2=80=99s conclusions and has quibbles with the process:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Between late 2019 and the end of 2=
020, Coinbase more than doubled the number of crypto assets available for t=
rading on the Coinbase Platform, and it more than doubled that number again=
 in 2021. During this period, Coinbase made available on the Coinbase Platf=
orm crypto assets with high =E2=80=9Crisk=E2=80=9D scores under the CRC fra=
mework it had adopted. In other words, to realize exponential growth of the=
 Coinbase Platform and boost its own trading profits, Coinbase made the str=
ategic business decision to add crypto assets to the Coinbase Platform even=
 where it recognized the crypto assets had the characteristics of securitie=
s.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Meanwhile here is how Binance=E2=80=99s wonder=
ful chief compliance officer described <em>his</em>=C2=A0fact-intensive ana=
lysis of whether Binance=C2=A0listed security tokens in the US:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As Binance=E2=80=99s CCO bluntly a=
dmitted to another Binance compliance officer in December 2018, <strong>=E2=
=80=9Cwe are operating as a fking unlicensed securities exchange in the USA=
 bro.</strong>=E2=80=9D (Emphasis added.)</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Just a much clearer perspective! Coinbase hire=
d a lot of lawyers and did a lot of analysis and wrote a lot of checklists =
to convince itself that it was legally running a crypto exchange in the US.=
 Binance was like =E2=80=9Cwell obviously this is illegal in the US, ah wel=
l.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0The SEC=C2=A0<em>absolutely agrees with Binance</em>.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This might work out well for Coinbase: It migh=
t be able to go to court and portray itself as a good actor who tried to fo=
llow the law, while Binance looks like a bad actor who tried to ignore the =
law; Coinbase might win against the SEC and Binance might lose. But I have =
to say that,=C2=A0<em>so far</em>, Binance=E2=80=99s approach seems smarter=
. (Though obviously don=E2=80=99t put it in writing, etc.)=C2=A0Binance not=
iced that it's illegal to run a crypto exchange in the US, and did it anywa=
y, but minimized and compartmentalized its US exposure: It has relatively f=
ew US customers and seems to keep most of its business out of the US. Coinb=
ase went all-in on the possibility of running a legal and regulatorily comp=
liant crypto exchange in the US, and now the SEC has said that that=E2=80=
=99s impossible. If the SEC is right, what is left for Coinbase?</p>

5px;">SEC v. Crypto: What is a security?</h2>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Okay let=E2=80=99s discuss the basic theory of=
 the SEC=E2=80=99s case here, which we previously <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31711038.259416/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJ=
nLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDQtMTcvdGhlLXNlYy1jb21lcy1mb3ItYml0dH=
JleD9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNjA2MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY=
2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA2MDYmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f=
07ceef4b8524a319a23cCc27f3b29" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscop=
e" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !import=
ant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-=
style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  discussed when the SEC sued Bittre=
x</a>:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">If you run an exchange that offers se=
curities trading in the US, you need to register it as a securities exchang=
e with the SEC.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Binance and Coinba=
se offer trading of crypto tokens that are securities.</li>=09<li style=3D"=
margin-bottom: 5px;">They did not register their US exchanges as securities=
 exchanges.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color:=
 #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; borde=
r-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-c=
olor: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>
</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Bad!</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Point 1 is a bit more nuanced than that; there=
 are, for instance, stock trading venues that are not registered as securit=
ies exchanges, though they are registered with the SEC under other rules. B=
ut from the SEC=E2=80=99s perspective the important thing here is that the =
rules for securities exchanges are designed to protect investors. In partic=
ular, they tend to require a separation of three key functions =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0the=C2=A0<em>exchange=C2=A0</em>that matches buyers and sellers, the=C2=
=A0<em>broker-dealer=C2=A0</em>that trades on the exchange on behalf of cus=
tomers, and the <em>clearinghouse </em>that actually moves the money and se=
curities =E2=80=94=C2=A0that are usually combined in crypto. In the stock m=
arket, you go to Robinhood=E2=80=99s website to put in an order to buy stoc=
k on the New York Stock Exchange,<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footn=
ote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bo=
ttom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !i=
mportant; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;"=
>
<span>[3]</span>
</a> and the Depository Trust Co. keeps custody of the stock and settles th=
e trade. In the crypto market, you go to Coinbase=E2=80=99s website to put =
in an order to buy crypto on Coinbase, and Coinbase keeps custody of the cr=
ypto and settles the trade.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But the main point here is Point 3: Are crypto=
 tokens securities?<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-4" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; ba=
ckground-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a>=C2=A0The SEC=E2=80=99s basic view is that most crypto tokens =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0not all of them, not Bitcoin, but most of them =E2=80=94=C2=A0are sec=
urities under US law. Coinbase=E2=80=99s view, certainly, is that lots of t=
hem aren=E2=80=99t. The particular question at issue here is whether a list=
 of popular crypto tokens =E2=80=94=C2=A0the SEC cites a bunch including So=
lana=E2=80=99s SOL token, Cardano=E2=80=99s ADA, Polygon=E2=80=99s MATIC, F=
ilecoin=E2=80=99s FIL, Decentraland=E2=80=99s MANA, Algorand=E2=80=99s ALGO=
, Axie Infinity=E2=80=99s AXS and Voyager Digital=E2=80=99s VGX =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0that were listed on Binance and/or Coinbase are securities.<a class=
=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-5" style=3D"color: #000000; text-deco=
ration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00=
0000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !=
important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[5]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusi=
ness.com/click/31711038.259416/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubGF3LmNvcm5lbGwuZWR1L3VzY29k=
ZS90ZXh0LzE1Lzc3Yg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB6d6d27ff" itemprop=3D"StoryLink=
" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-d=
ecoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: =
#000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> US securitie=
s laws</a> define a =E2=80=9Csecurity=E2=80=9D to include, among other thin=
gs,=C2=A0any =E2=80=9Cstock,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9Ccertificate of interest or p=
articipation in any profit-sharing agreement,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9Cpreorganiza=
tion certificate or subscription,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9Ctransferable share, inv=
estment contract, [or] voting-trust=C2=A0certificate.=E2=80=9D The most gen=
eral term there is =E2=80=9Cinvestment contract,=E2=80=9D and the US Suprem=
e Court explained it in a famous 1946 case called <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31711038.259416/aHR0cHM6Ly9zdXByZW1lLmp1c3R=
pYS5jb20vY2FzZXMvZmVkZXJhbC91cy8zMjgvMjkzLw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB7fee0e=
40" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;"> SEC v. W.J. Howey Co.</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">An investment contract, for purposes of the Se=
curities Act, means a contract, transaction=C2=A0or scheme whereby a person=
 invests his money in a common enterprise and is led to expect profits sole=
ly from the efforts of the promoter or a third party, it being immaterial w=
hether the shares in the enterprise are evidenced by formal certificates or=
 by nominal interests in the physical assets employed in the enterprise. Su=
ch a definition =E2=80=A6 permits the fulfillment of the statutory purpose =
of compelling full and fair disclosure relative to the issuance of =E2=80=
=9Cthe many types of instruments that, in our commercial world, fall within=
 the ordinary concept of a security.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6 It embodies a flexi=
ble, rather than a static, principle, one that is capable of adaptation to =
meet the countless and variable schemes devised by those who seek the use o=
f the money of others on the promise of profits. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The investors provide the capital and share in=
 the earnings and profits; the promoters manage, control, and operate the e=
nterprise. It follows that the arrangements whereby the investors' interest=
s are made manifest involve investment contracts, regardless of the legal t=
erminology in which such contracts are clothed. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The test is whether the scheme involves an inv=
estment of money in a common enterprise with profits to come solely from th=
e efforts of others. If that test be satisfied, it is immaterial whether th=
e enterprise is speculative or nonspeculative, or whether there is a sale o=
f property with or without intrinsic value.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This has become the =E2=80=9CHowey test,=E2=80=
=9D and courts ask whether there is (1) an investment of money (2) in a com=
mon enterprise (3) with an expectation of profits (4) to come solely from t=
he efforts of others. The SEC <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusines=
s.com/click/31711038.259416/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9jb3JwZmluL2ZyYW1ld29=
yay1pbnZlc3RtZW50LWNvbnRyYWN0LWFuYWx5c2lzLWRpZ2l0YWwtYXNzZXRz/607f07ceef4b8=
524a319a23cB698f2ac2" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;"> has argued</a>, at least <a href=3D"https://=
link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31711038.259416/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjL=
mdvdi9saXRpZ2F0aW9uL2ludmVzdHJlcG9ydC8zNC04MTIwNy5wZGY/607f07ceef4b8524a319=
a23cB8de6f17a" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_bl=
ank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; ba=
ckground-color: #ccc;"> since 2017</a>, that most crypto enterprises fit th=
is description.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">For instance, consider Solana. Solana is a blo=
ckchain that runs crypto applications; its native token is called SOL.=C2=
=A0Here=E2=80=99s the SEC=E2=80=99s explanation of Solana<a class=3D"footno=
te-index" href=3D"#footnote-6" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: no=
ne !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bord=
er-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important;=
 display: inline-block;">
<span>[6]</span>
</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CSOL=E2=80=9D is the native token of t=
he Solana blockchain. The Solana blockchain was created by Solana Labs, Inc=
. (=E2=80=9CSolana Labs=E2=80=9D), a Delaware corporation headquartered in =
San Francisco that was founded in 2018 by Anatoly Yakovenko (=E2=80=9CYakov=
enko=E2=80=9D) and Raj Gokal (Solana Labs=E2=80=99 current CEO and COO, res=
pectively). According to Solana=E2=80=99s website, www.solana.com, the Sola=
na blockchain is a network upon which decentralized apps (=E2=80=9CdApps=E2=
=80=9D) can be built, and is comprised of a platform that aims to improve b=
lockchain scalability and achieve high transaction speeds by using a combin=
ation of consensus mechanisms.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">According to Solana=E2=80=99s website, SOL may=
 be =E2=80=9Cstaked=E2=80=9D on the Solana blockchain to earn rewards, and =
a certain infinitesimal amount of SOL must be =E2=80=9Cburned=E2=80=9D to p=
ropose a transaction on the Solana blockchain, a common function for native=
 tokens on blockchains that constitutes a method for cryptographically dist=
ributed ledgers to avoid a potential bad actor from =E2=80=9Cspamming=E2=80=
=9D a blockchain by overwhelming it with an infinite number of proposed tra=
nsactions.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Solana Labs sold SOL tokens to raise money to =
build the Solana ecosystem:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Solana Labs stated publicly that i=
t would pool the proceeds from its private and public SOL sales in omnibus =
crypto asset wallets that it controlled, and that it would use those procee=
ds to fund the development, operations, and marketing efforts with respect =
to the Solana blockchain in order to attract more users to that blockchain =
(potentially increasing the demand for,=C2=A0and therefore the value of, SO=
L itself, given the need for those who wish to interact with the Solana blo=
ckchain to tender SOL). For example, in connection with the 2021 private sa=
le of SOL, Solana Labs stated publicly that it would use investor funds to:=
 (i) hire engineers and support staff to help grow Solana=E2=80=99s develop=
er ecosystem; (ii) =E2=80=9Caccelerate the deployment of market-ready appli=
cations focused on onboarding the next billion users into crypto=E2=80=9D; =
(iii) =E2=80=9Claunch an incubation studio to accelerate the development of=
 decentralized applications and Platforms building on Solana=E2=80=9D; and =
(iv) develop a =E2=80=9Cventure investing arm=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Ctrading=
 desk dedicated to the Solana ecosystem.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Howey test:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Did investors invest money? Yes, SOL =
was sold for money, to raise funding to build Solana.<a class=3D"footnote-i=
ndex" href=3D"#footnote-7" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !=
important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-b=
ottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; dis=
play: inline-block;">
<span>[7]</span>
</a>
</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Was there a common enterprise? Ye=
s, Solana is an=C2=A0<em>enterprise</em>; it is a blockchain ecosystem that=
 competes with Ethereum and Cardano and so forth, that tries to attract use=
rs.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Is there an expectation of pro=
fits? Yes, people bought SOL hoping that it would go up, and it did.=C2=A0<=
/li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Do the profits come from the effor=
ts of others? Yes, SOL went up because its promoters and developers built S=
olana into a popular blockchain, as they said they would, increasing the de=
mand for SOL.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">These are often close questions. Most big cryp=
to blockchains are to some extent decentralized; Solana=E2=80=99s growth de=
pends not only on the efforts of Solana Labs but also on the efforts of thi=
rd-party users and developers who like using it.<a class=3D"footnote-index"=
 href=3D"#footnote-8" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impor=
tant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom=
-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display:=
 inline-block;">
<span>[8]</span>
</a> With some crypto tokens, it is plausible to argue that people buy the =
tokens=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>as an investment with an expectation of profi=
ts, but rather to pay for transactions on the blockchain; the SOL token is =
the =E2=80=9Cgas=E2=80=9D that people use to run programs and transactions =
on the Solana blockchain, and if you buy SOL as a pure =E2=80=9Cutility tok=
en=E2=80=9D then it is arguably not a security. Most crypto tokens <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31711038.259416/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDktMDgvZ2FyeS1nZW5z=
bGVyLXdhbnRzLXRvLXJlZ3VsYXRlLWNyeXB0bz9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNjA2MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ=
1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA2MDYmdX=
RtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB69dcd58d" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
  have=C2=A0<em>both=C2=A0</em>utility=C2=A0<em>and=C2=A0</em>speculative-i=
nvestment=C2=A0aspects</a>, muddying the analysis a bit.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also, even if you buy SOL as a speculative inv=
estment, it is not clear that you are buying it to=C2=A0<em>share in the pr=
ofits of the underlying enterprise.=C2=A0</em>Your thought process might be=
 =E2=80=9Cif I buy SOL, a lot of other people might buy SOL, and its price =
will go up and I will make money.=E2=80=9D That is not really an expectatio=
n of profits derived from the =E2=80=9Cefforts of others=E2=80=9D; that=E2=
=80=99s an expectation of profits derived from speculative mania and online=
 memes. We <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/3171103=
8.259416/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMD=
YtMDUvZWxvbi1tdXNrLWhhZC1mdW4td2l0aC1kb2dlY29pbj9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNjA2MjNfTU9OR=
VlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0y=
MzA2MDYmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBefeb0974" =
itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"c=
olor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; =
border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color:=
 #ccc;">  talked yesterday about Dogecoin</a>, a joke crypto token where ev=
eryone pretty ostentatiously promised to do nothing at all to build an ecos=
ystem; people buy Dogecoin because they think other people will buy Dogecoi=
n. I=C2=A0<em>think=C2=A0</em>that that means that Dogecoin is <em>not </em=
>a security; a purely meaningless token doesn=E2=80=99t involve enough effo=
rts-of-others to qualify.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-9" s=
tyle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wid=
th: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important=
; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[9]</span>
</a>=C2=A0(Surely, e.g., Beanie Babies are not securities.) And surely even=
 when a crypto project=C2=A0<em>does=C2=A0</em>promise efforts and ecosyste=
ms and hard-working brilliant developers, a lot of people probably buy the =
tokens for the same pure number-go-up reasons that they bought Dogecoin.=C2=
=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But the way that I like to think of crypto and=
 securities is that most crypto tokens are pretty clearly analogues of=C2=
=A0<em>stock=C2=A0</em>in some underlying=C2=A0<em>tech business</em>. In S=
olana (and Cardano, Polygon, etc.), the underlying business is a platform b=
usiness, a blockchain ecosystem for building apps, largely though not exclu=
sively financial-services apps for trading=C2=A0crypto. And everyone who bu=
ys the tokens is in some loose sense a quasi-shareholder of the business. A=
nd they are rewarded in the same way that traditional shareholders are: wit=
h stock buybacks.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-10" style=3D=
"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px=
; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; backg=
round-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[10]</span>
</a>=C2=A0The crypto term for stock buybacks is =E2=80=9Cburning=E2=80=9D:<=
/p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Further, Solana Labs markets that =
it =E2=80=9Cburns=E2=80=9D (or destroys) SOL tokens as part of a =E2=80=9Cd=
eflationary model.=E2=80=9D As Yakovenko explained in an April 14, 2021 art=
icle entitled =E2=80=9CSolana (SOL): Scaling Crypto to the Masses=E2=80=9D =
posted on gemini.com, =E2=80=9CSolana transaction fees are paid in SOL and=
=C2=A0burnt (or permanently destroyed) as a deflationary mechanism to reduc=
e the total supply and thereby maintain a healthy SOL price.=E2=80=9D As ex=
plained on the Solana website, since the Solana network was launched, the =
=E2=80=9CTotal Current Supply=E2=80=9D of SOL =E2=80=9Chas been reduced by =
the burning of transaction fees and a planned token reduction event.=E2=80=
=9D This marketed burning of SOL as part of the Solana network=E2=80=99s =
=E2=80=9Cdeflationary mechanism=E2=80=9D has led investors reasonably to vi=
ew their purchase of SOL as having the potential for profit to the extent t=
here is a built-in mechanism to decrease the supply and therefore increase =
the price of SOL.</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In the <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=
usiness.com/click/31711038.259416/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9maWxlcy9saXRpZ=
2F0aW9uL2NvbXBsYWludHMvMjAyMy9jb21wLXByMjAyMy0xMDEucGRm/607f07ceef4b8524a31=
9a23cC0f866a2a" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_b=
lank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bot=
tom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; b=
ackground-color: #ccc;"> Binance complaint</a>, the SEC quotes Changpeng Zh=
ao describing the burning mechanism for Binance=E2=80=99s own BNB token:=C2=
=A0</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Indeed, on July 9, 2019, Zhao desc=
ribed Binance=E2=80=99s planned BNB burn in an interview posted on YouTube,=
 stating that a =E2=80=9Cbenefit we promised in the Whitepaper is every qua=
rter we will use 20 percent of our profits to buy back [BNB] at the market =
value =E2=80=A6 we will buy back and destroy those. We will destroy up to 1=
00 million Binance coins. Basically half of all available coins =E2=80=A6 F=
inancially that works the same way as a dividend economically.=E2=80=9D</p>=
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I mean I would say it works the same as a stoc=
k buyback, but sure, dividends and stock buybacks are essentially equivalen=
t. The point is that shareholders =E2=80=94=C2=A0sorry, tokenholders =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0in a crypto project share in the profits of that project when it u=
ses some of its revenue to buy back and burn tokens, increasing the value o=
f the remaining tokens, exactly the way a share buyback works.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One way to understand crypto economics is that=
 crypto built a new way to <em>sell stock=C2=A0</em>in promising technology=
 and financial businesses=C2=A0<em>without calling it stock</em>. If you ar=
e starting a crypto exchange, for instance, and you want to raise money, yo=
u could go to investors and offer them stock in your business. If the busin=
ess does well, there will be lots of profits (from charging customers tradi=
ng fees), and you will share them with your investors. But this has problem=
s:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">If you sell stock to the public, you =
will need to register with the SEC.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;=
">If you sell stock to big venture capitalists,=C2=A0<em>they </em>will nee=
d to register their resales with the SEC, or otherwise find an exemption fr=
om SEC registration.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Either way yo=
u will probably have to give your investors=C2=A0<em>some=C2=A0</em>sort of=
 financial information about the business to get their money.</li>=09<li st=
yle=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Your shareholders might want voting rights, ong=
oing financial disclosures, etc., which are customary and often mandated by=
 law.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Or, if you are starting a crypto exchange, you=
 can go to investors and offer them=C2=A0<em>tokens=C2=A0</em>in your busin=
ess. If the business does well, there will be lots of profits (from chargin=
g customers trading fees), and you will share them with your investors (by =
buying and burning tokens). This is very nice:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">If you sell tokens to the public, you=
 can say that they are not securities, and not register with the SEC.</li>=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">If you sell the tokens to big VCS,=C2=
=A0<em>they=C2=A0</em>can say that they are not securities, and resell them=
 freely.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You will write a white pa=
per to sell the tokens that does not necessarily get into a ton of financia=
l or operational detail.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You can g=
ive the tokens whatever rights you want.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I am being a little unfair. I am describing a =
pure regulatory arbitrage; Binance=E2=80=99s BNB token =E2=80=94=C2=A0or <a=
 href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31711038.259416/aHR0=
cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMTEtMTQvZnR4LXM=
tYmFsYW5jZS1zaGVldC13YXMtYmFkP2NtcGlkPUJCRDA2MDYyM19NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZW=
RpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIzMDYwNiZ1dG1fY2Ftc=
GFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB617b95a3" itemprop=3D"StoryL=
ink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; tex=
t-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-colo=
r: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  FTX=E2=
=80=99s FTT token</a> =E2=80=94=C2=A0is a pure substitute for stock, issued=
 by a company to raise money to run its centralized business. Lots of crypt=
o is not quite like this; in some projects <a href=3D"https://link.mail.blo=
ombergbusiness.com/click/31711038.259416/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS=
9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMTctMTAtMTMvaWNvcy1tYXJ4aXNtLWFuZC1jcmVkaXQtcmVwb=
3J0cz9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNjA2MjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3Vy=
Y2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA2MDYmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607=
f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB6d254bd5" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsco=
pe" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impor=
tant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom=
-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  there is a real idealism</a> abou=
t building decentralized ecosystems that will not be owned by any one compa=
ny, and selling tokens can be a way to create and fund projects without own=
ers, an economic model that really is very different from a shareholder-own=
ed corporation. But much of the time, the crypto ecosystems seem to be buil=
t by pretty centralized teams, and the tokens are sold as more or less stoc=
k in a new tech company with a promising idea started by a promising team.<=
/p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">You can see why people in crypto like this! It=
 combines regulatory arbitrage with a certain exciting philosophical newfan=
gledness. You can see why the SEC does not like it! The SEC knows all about=
 =E2=80=9C=C2=A0the countless and variable schemes devised by those who see=
k the use of the money of others on the promise of profits.=E2=80=9D The SE=
C is the regulator getting arbitraged. And it is not happy about it.</p>

olor: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> binary=
 options or contracts for differences</a>. Either way it lives on abroad, b=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I don=E2=80=99t know which outcome I would bet=
 on. The last outcome is the one that the crypto industry wants, and there =
does seem to be <em>some=C2=A0</em>appetite in Congress to write crypto rul=
es.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But I do want to say that <em>the SEC </em>is =
clearly betting on the first outcome. That is why it is bringing these case=
s=C2=A0<em>now</em>, after the collapse of FTX and so many other big crypto=
 firms, after crypto prices have fallen, after the venture capitalists have=
 all moved on to AI. These cases, against Binance and Coinbase, are high-ri=
sk cases for the SEC: Coinbase and Bitcoin are big well-funded companies=C2=
=A0with good lawyers and lobbyists,=C2=A0they have the resources and motiva=
tion to fight these cases to the end, and they do have decent legal argumen=
ts. The SEC might lose! But it is being strategic about maximizing its chan=
ces. I <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31711038.25=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When crypto is popular and exciting and going =
up, if you are a regulator who says =E2=80=9Cno, we must stop this,=E2=80=
=9D you look like a killjoy. Investors want to put their money into stuff t=
hat is going up, and they are mad at you for stopping them. Politicians lik=
e the stuff that is going up, and hold hearings about how you=E2=80=99re st=
ifling innovation. Crypto founders are rich and popular and criticize you o=
n Twitter and get a lot of likes and retweets. Your own regulatory employee=
s, who have an eye on their next private-sector jobs, want to be leaders in=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When crypto is going down and so many projects=
 are evaporating in fraud and bankruptcy, you can kind of say =E2=80=9CI to=
ld you so.=E2=80=9D There is just a lot more appetite to regulate, or I gue=
ss just to shut everything down. =E2=80=9CYou are stifling innovation,=E2=
=80=9D the indicted founder of a bankrupt crypto firm can say, but nobody c=
ares.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That is the bet that the SEC is making. Now we=
=E2=80=99ll see if it=E2=80=99s right.</p>
